questions.question: Explain the difference between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling. Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Preemptive allows forced removal of CPU from running thread
Non-preemptive lets thread run until completion or voluntary yield
Round Robin is preemptive (time quantum based)
FCFS is non-preemptive example

questions.question: Define thread contention scope (TCS) and distinguish between Process Contention Scope and System Contention Scope.
keyPoints: TCS defines competition boundaries for threads
PCS is competition among threads within same process
SCS is competition among all threads in the system
PCS typically used by thread libraries, SCS by kernel

questions.question: What are the main scheduling criteria used to evaluate thread scheduling algorithms?
keyPoints: CPU Utilization - keep CPU busy
Throughput - completions per time unit
Turnaround Time - submission to completion
Waiting Time - time in ready queue
Response Time - first CPU response

questions.question: Explain the convoy effect in FCFS scheduling with an example.
keyPoints: Convoy effect occurs when short threads wait behind long ones
Example: one long thread (100ms) ahead of several short threads (1ms each)
Poor average waiting time despite simple implementation
Also called helicopter effect

questions.question: What is processor affinity? Explain its importance in multiprocessor scheduling.
keyPoints: Processor affinity binds threads to specific CPUs
Improves cache performance by keeping data in local cache
Two types: soft (preferred) and hard (mandatory)
Trade-off between cache locality and load balancing

questions.question: Describe the Round Robin scheduling algorithm and discuss how the time quantum affects its performance.
keyPoints: Time slices assigned cyclically to each thread
Very small quantum increases context switches overhead
Very large quantum makes it behave like FCFS
Optimal quantum balances responsiveness and overhead

questions.question: What is thread starvation? Under what conditions does it occur and how can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Starvation occurs when thread perpetually denied CPU
Common in priority scheduling - low priority threads wait indefinitely
Prevention: aging (increase priority over time)
Also possible in Multilevel Queue if queues get starved

questions.question: Compare and contrast user-level threads and kernel-level threads. Explain how thread scheduling differs for each type.
keyPoints: User-level: managed by library, fast creation, no kernel awareness, cannot utilize multiple CPUs
Kernel-level: OS managed, slower but can use multiple CPUs, one thread blocking can block entire process
User-level scheduling done by library (PCS)
Kernel-level scheduling uses SCS from common ready queue
Hybrid approaches combine benefits of both

questions.question: Discuss the various thread scheduling algorithms in detail with their advantages, disadvantages, and suitable use cases.
keyPoints: FCFS: Simple, convoy effect, batch processing
SJF: Minimum waiting time, requires burst prediction, starvation
Round Robin: Fair, good for time-sharing, depends on quantum
Priority: Real-time capable, starvation issue
Multilevel Queue: Handles diverse thread types, complex
Multilevel Feedback Queue: Adaptive, complex implementation
Discuss time complexity and suitability for different scenarios

questions.question: Explain multiple-processor scheduling. Discuss symmetric multiprocessing, load balancing, and processor affinity.
keyPoints: SMP: All CPUs equal, common ready queue
Load balancing: Push (periodic redistribution) and Pull (idle CPUs take work)
Processor affinity: Soft (preferred) vs Hard (mandatory) binding
Cache coherence issues with migration
Trade-off between migration (load balance) and affinity (cache)
NUMA systems add complexity with memory access times

questions.question: What is multilevel queue scheduling? Explain with a practical example and discuss its advantages and limitations.
keyPoints: Multiple queues with different scheduling algorithms
Queue assignment based on thread properties
Example: System processes (RR), Interactive (RR), Batch (FCFS)
Fixed priority between queues or time slicing between queues
Advantages: Handles diverse thread types efficiently
Limitations: Lower queues may starve, rigid design
